title: Male Urinary Catheterisation
oneLiner: A sterile procedure to drain the bladder via the urethra in men with acute retention, monitoring needs, or obstruction – trauma & infection are the main risks.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Urethral rupture / false passage** — paraphimosis, blood at the meatus, or a catheter that passes easily but yields no urine (tip in false tract) → **stop immediately**, do not inflate balloon; call urology / senior support
- **Catheter‑associated sepsis** — rigors, hypotension, high fever within 60 minutes of insertion → **start broad‑spectrum IV antibiotics** (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam [check local policy]) and escalate to ICU if shock develops; do not wait for cultures
- **Acute kidney injury from prolonged obstruction** — known chronic retention with new anuria, or post‑obstructive diuresis after draining >1 litre → **monitor fluid balance hourly and check serum creatinine / potassium**; consider IV fluids if polyuria >200 ml/hr
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** inability to pass urine despite a full bladder (acute retention), dribbling incontinence (overflow), suprapubic pain or distension, urethral trauma (blood at meatus)
- **Key exam findings:** palpable suprapubic mass (bladder >15 cm above pubic symphysis), dull to percussion; if chronic retention → bilateral renal angle tenderness, hypertension, or signs of uraemia (confusion, asterixis); urethral meatal or scrotal swelling / haematoma suggests trauma
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute urinary retention (AUR)** → most common; bladder scan >500 ml, pain, patient straining
- **Chronic retention → overflow incontinence** → same bladder volume but no pain, often with hypertension and uraemia; differentiate by history of “dribbling” and loss of sensation
- **Anuria due to renal failure** → no suprapubic distension, bladder scan <50 ml; check serum creatinine & potassium urgently
- **Low‑outflow state / pre‑renal AKI** → hypotension, dry mucous membranes, low urine output but no bladder distension; respond to fluid challenge
- **Neurogenic bladder (e.g. cauda equina)** → saddle anaesthesia, loss of anal tone, urinary retention with normal bladder scan volume; needs urgent MRI
- **Splitter:** “Suprapubic pain + bladder distension + new retention” = AUR; “No pain + bladder distension + serum creatinine elevated” = chronic retention with obstruction
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scanner – to confirm volume and position (must be >500 ml for retention – beware of under‑scanning if patient is obese or catheterised earlier)
  - Urine dipstick (infection / haematuria)
  - Urethral swab if suspected STI (optional before catheterisation if feasible – do not delay in acute retention)
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine – baseline for obstruction
  - FBC, CRP – infection marker
  - Coagulation screen if on anticoagulation or suspected bleeding
- **Imaging:**
  - No routine imaging before simple catheterisation
  - Renal ultrasound if chronic retention or elevated creatinine – look for hydronephrosis
  - Urethrogram / cystoscopy if trauma or failed catheterisation
- **Special tests:** None routinely
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** None – diagnosis is clinical
- **Never-miss test:** **Bladder scan** – omitting this before catheterisation risks missing a neurogenic bladder (low volume) or confirming retention; also avoids unnecessary catheterisation in anuria from renal failure
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess for contraindications:** blood at meatus, scrotal haematoma → do NOT catheterise; call urology for suprapubic route
  - **I nformed consent** – explain risks (pain, infection, haematuria, trauma, retention)
  - **Sterile technique** – hand scrub, sterile gloves, drapes, lubricant (2% lidocaine gel 10–20 ml instilled into urethra, wait 2–5 minutes for anaesthesia)
  - **Catheter selection:** 14 Fr (standard adult male), 12 Fr if small, 16–20 Fr if haematuria / large clots; balloon volume = 10 ml sterile water (no larger unless 20 ml balloon – then fill to 10–15 ml to prevent trauma)
  - **Insertion technique:**
    - Hold penis at 90° to body, gentle traction
    - Advance catheter until urine flows, then advance another 5–7 cm to ensure balloon is fully in bladder
    - Inflate balloon with *sterile water* (never air or saline) – patient should feel no pain; if pain, stop – balloon may be in urethra → deflate, withdraw, reposition
    - Secure catheter with drainage bag (leg bag or night bag) – free drainage initially
  - **Post‑insertion:** Measure urine output, send for MSU if infection suspected; check bladder scan if no output – rule out blockage / kink / false passage
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor urine output hourly x 4 hours then 4‑hourly if stable
  - Consider catheter change once resolved (e.g., if <5 days, same catheter); for long‑term use plan suprapubic change every 4–6 weeks
  - If post‑obstructive diuresis (output >200 ml/h for >4 hours) → start IV crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s) 1 ml per 1 ml urine output until polyuria settles; check U&Es every 6–12 hours
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - If catheter stays >48 hours, plan for removal trial (clamp for 2–4 hours, then remove if voiding >200 ml)
  - If long term (e.g., spinal cord injury / neurogenic bladder) → change every 4–6 weeks by district nurse or GP; patient may self‑catheterise if able
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: nil unless retention >1L or post‑obstructive diuresis – then Hartmann’s 1 ml / ml is standard
  - VTE prophylaxis: all admitted patients with catheter – enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily [check local weight‑based policy]
  - Diet/NBM: oral intake as tolerated – no restriction unless post‑op
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 15 minutes for 1 hour post‑catheter, then 4‑hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Lidocaine 2% gel (Instillagel)** – 10–20 ml urethral instillation before catheter – no systemic absorption
  - **Catheter balloon fill:** 10 ml sterile water (no additives)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Plain lubricant** if allergic to lidocaine – use lignocaine‑free gel (e.g., KY jelly) but it offers no anaesthesia – patient may require sedation or regional block
  - **Antibiotics – prophylactic only** for high‑risk patients (prosthetic heart valve, recent joint replacement, known bacteriuria, immunocompromised) → **single dose gentamicin 2–3 mg/kg IV** [check local policy] or **co‑amoxiclav 1.2 g IV** for penicillin allergy – consult microbiology
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Lidocaine gel – rare allergy; avoid in severe bradycardia or second‑degree heart block if large dose absorbed (unlikely with 20 ml gel)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for topical lidocaine gel; others as per local guidelines
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Inflating balloon with >10 ml water or using air instead of water** – air can cause bladder rupture or embolus; overinflation leads to intra‑urethral balloon inflation → urethral trauma
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Urethral trauma / false passage** – most common; often due to excess force or balloon inflation in urethra – stop, deflate, call senior
  - **Catheter‑associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI)** – 3–7% per day; avoid prolonged use; remove as soon as possible
  - **Haematuria** – often mild; if heavy → check clotting, consider bladder irrigation (3‑way catheter, 1 L normal saline, 3 L per day rate)
  - **Acute urine retention due to overfilled balloon** – balloon may be larger than 10 ml but pulled into bladder neck; deflate to 10 ml
- **Chronic:**
  - **Urethral stricture** – repeated catheterisations
  - **Bladder stones / chronic infection** from long‑term indwelling catheters
  - **Catheter encrustation / blockage** – change every 4–6 weeks; consider silver‑coated or silicone catheters
sections.Prognosis: - **Excellent** for most men with acute retention once catheter drained – 80% will void normally within 48 hours after removal; chronic retention carries a higher risk of recurrent obstruction, infection, and renal impairment (5% progress to end‑stage renal disease)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use lidocaine gel and wait 2–5 minutes** – reduces pain and urethral spasm; never insert a dry catheter
- **After urine flows, advance another 5–7 cm** before inflating balloon – this is the single most important step to avoid balloon inflation in the urethra
- **If at first you don’t succeed, call for help** – repeated failed attempts increase trauma; a single experienced attempt (urology / senior nurse) often succeeds
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not inflate the balloon until you are certain it is in the bladder** – if patient feels a sharp pain, deflate immediately and withdraw
- **Do not use a catheter >16 Fr for routine insertion** – larger sizes cause more trauma, especially in men with prostates
- **Do not leave a catheter in situ for longer than necessary** – remove within 48 hours unless specifically indicated; prolonged use is the main driver of CAUTI and antibiotic resistance
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Catheter stops draining urine (blockage or kink) – do not attempt to flush yourself; come to ED
  - Fever (>38°C) or rigors – urinary sepsis; require IV antibiotics
  - Heavy bleeding (clots, haematuria) – especially if taking anticoagulants
- **Follow-up:** GP or Urology clinic in 2 weeks if catheter removal planned; if long‑term, district nurse in 1 week for re‑training
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / urology:**
  - Unable to pass catheter despite two attempts by an experienced clinician
  - Blood at meatus or scrotal haematoma before any attempt – do not catheterise; suprapubic route indicated
  - Suspected false passage – stop, deflate, call for help
  - Patient develops severe hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) or NEWS2 ≥5 within 30 minutes – start antibiotics, ECG, IV fluids
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
  - Anaphylaxis to lidocaine (rare – facial swelling, wheeze, hypotension)
  - Cardiac arrest due to severe sepsis or electrolyte disturbance after obstructed uropathy (hyperkalaemia)

rawMarkdown:
## Male Urinary Catheterisation
**One-liner:** A sterile procedure to drain the bladder via the urethra in men with acute retention, monitoring needs, or obstruction – trauma & infection are the main risks.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Urethral rupture / false passage** — paraphimosis, blood at the meatus, or a catheter that passes easily but yields no urine (tip in false tract) → **stop immediately**, do not inflate balloon; call urology / senior support
- **Catheter‑associated sepsis** — rigors, hypotension, high fever within 60 minutes of insertion → **start broad‑spectrum IV antibiotics** (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam [check local policy]) and escalate to ICU if shock develops; do not wait for cultures
- **Acute kidney injury from prolonged obstruction** — known chronic retention with new anuria, or post‑obstructive diuresis after draining >1 litre → **monitor fluid balance hourly and check serum creatinine / potassium**; consider IV fluids if polyuria >200 ml/hr

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** inability to pass urine despite a full bladder (acute retention), dribbling incontinence (overflow), suprapubic pain or distension, urethral trauma (blood at meatus)
- **Key exam findings:** palpable suprapubic mass (bladder >15 cm above pubic symphysis), dull to percussion; if chronic retention → bilateral renal angle tenderness, hypertension, or signs of uraemia (confusion, asterixis); urethral meatal or scrotal swelling / haematoma suggests trauma

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute urinary retention (AUR)** → most common; bladder scan >500 ml, pain, patient straining
- **Chronic retention → overflow incontinence** → same bladder volume but no pain, often with hypertension and uraemia; differentiate by history of “dribbling” and loss of sensation
- **Anuria due to renal failure** → no suprapubic distension, bladder scan <50 ml; check serum creatinine & potassium urgently
- **Low‑outflow state / pre‑renal AKI** → hypotension, dry mucous membranes, low urine output but no bladder distension; respond to fluid challenge
- **Neurogenic bladder (e.g. cauda equina)** → saddle anaesthesia, loss of anal tone, urinary retention with normal bladder scan volume; needs urgent MRI
- **Splitter:** “Suprapubic pain + bladder distension + new retention” = AUR; “No pain + bladder distension + serum creatinine elevated” = chronic retention with obstruction

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scanner – to confirm volume and position (must be >500 ml for retention – beware of under‑scanning if patient is obese or catheterised earlier)
  - Urine dipstick (infection / haematuria)
  - Urethral swab if suspected STI (optional before catheterisation if feasible – do not delay in acute retention)
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine – baseline for obstruction
  - FBC, CRP – infection marker
  - Coagulation screen if on anticoagulation or suspected bleeding
- **Imaging:**
  - No routine imaging before simple catheterisation
  - Renal ultrasound if chronic retention or elevated creatinine – look for hydronephrosis
  - Urethrogram / cystoscopy if trauma or failed catheterisation
- **Special tests:** None routinely
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** None – diagnosis is clinical
- **Never-miss test:** **Bladder scan** – omitting this before catheterisation risks missing a neurogenic bladder (low volume) or confirming retention; also avoids unnecessary catheterisation in anuria from renal failure

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess for contraindications:** blood at meatus, scrotal haematoma → do NOT catheterise; call urology for suprapubic route
  - **I nformed consent** – explain risks (pain, infection, haematuria, trauma, retention)
  - **Sterile technique** – hand scrub, sterile gloves, drapes, lubricant (2% lidocaine gel 10–20 ml instilled into urethra, wait 2–5 minutes for anaesthesia)
  - **Catheter selection:** 14 Fr (standard adult male), 12 Fr if small, 16–20 Fr if haematuria / large clots; balloon volume = 10 ml sterile water (no larger unless 20 ml balloon – then fill to 10–15 ml to prevent trauma)
  - **Insertion technique:**
    - Hold penis at 90° to body, gentle traction
    - Advance catheter until urine flows, then advance another 5–7 cm to ensure balloon is fully in bladder
    - Inflate balloon with *sterile water* (never air or saline) – patient should feel no pain; if pain, stop – balloon may be in urethra → deflate, withdraw, reposition
    - Secure catheter with drainage bag (leg bag or night bag) – free drainage initially
  - **Post‑insertion:** Measure urine output, send for MSU if infection suspected; check bladder scan if no output – rule out blockage / kink / false passage
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor urine output hourly x 4 hours then 4‑hourly if stable
  - Consider catheter change once resolved (e.g., if <5 days, same catheter); for long‑term use plan suprapubic change every 4–6 weeks
  - If post‑obstructive diuresis (output >200 ml/h for >4 hours) → start IV crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s) 1 ml per 1 ml urine output until polyuria settles; check U&Es every 6–12 hours
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - If catheter stays >48 hours, plan for removal trial (clamp for 2–4 hours, then remove if voiding >200 ml)
  - If long term (e.g., spinal cord injury / neurogenic bladder) → change every 4–6 weeks by district nurse or GP; patient may self‑catheterise if able
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: nil unless retention >1L or post‑obstructive diuresis – then Hartmann’s 1 ml / ml is standard
  - VTE prophylaxis: all admitted patients with catheter – enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily [check local weight‑based policy]
  - Diet/NBM: oral intake as tolerated – no restriction unless post‑op
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 15 minutes for 1 hour post‑catheter, then 4‑hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Lidocaine 2% gel (Instillagel)** – 10–20 ml urethral instillation before catheter – no systemic absorption
  - **Catheter balloon fill:** 10 ml sterile water (no additives)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Plain lubricant** if allergic to lidocaine – use lignocaine‑free gel (e.g., KY jelly) but it offers no anaesthesia – patient may require sedation or regional block
  - **Antibiotics – prophylactic only** for high‑risk patients (prosthetic heart valve, recent joint replacement, known bacteriuria, immunocompromised) → **single dose gentamicin 2–3 mg/kg IV** [check local policy] or **co‑amoxiclav 1.2 g IV** for penicillin allergy – consult microbiology
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Lidocaine gel – rare allergy; avoid in severe bradycardia or second‑degree heart block if large dose absorbed (unlikely with 20 ml gel)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for topical lidocaine gel; others as per local guidelines
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Inflating balloon with >10 ml water or using air instead of water** – air can cause bladder rupture or embolus; overinflation leads to intra‑urethral balloon inflation → urethral trauma

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Urethral trauma / false passage** – most common; often due to excess force or balloon inflation in urethra – stop, deflate, call senior
  - **Catheter‑associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI)** – 3–7% per day; avoid prolonged use; remove as soon as possible
  - **Haematuria** – often mild; if heavy → check clotting, consider bladder irrigation (3‑way catheter, 1 L normal saline, 3 L per day rate)
  - **Acute urine retention due to overfilled balloon** – balloon may be larger than 10 ml but pulled into bladder neck; deflate to 10 ml
- **Chronic:**
  - **Urethral stricture** – repeated catheterisations
  - **Bladder stones / chronic infection** from long‑term indwelling catheters
  - **Catheter encrustation / blockage** – change every 4–6 weeks; consider silver‑coated or silicone catheters

### Prognosis
- **Excellent** for most men with acute retention once catheter drained – 80% will void normally within 48 hours after removal; chronic retention carries a higher risk of recurrent obstruction, infection, and renal impairment (5% progress to end‑stage renal disease)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use lidocaine gel and wait 2–5 minutes** – reduces pain and urethral spasm; never insert a dry catheter
- **After urine flows, advance another 5–7 cm** before inflating balloon – this is the single most important step to avoid balloon inflation in the urethra
- **If at first you don’t succeed, call for help** – repeated failed attempts increase trauma; a single experienced attempt (urology / senior nurse) often succeeds

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not inflate the balloon until you are certain it is in the bladder** – if patient feels a sharp pain, deflate immediately and withdraw
- **Do not use a catheter >16 Fr for routine insertion** – larger sizes cause more trauma, especially in men with prostates
- **Do not leave a catheter in situ for longer than necessary** – remove within 48 hours unless specifically indicated; prolonged use is the main driver of CAUTI and antibiotic resistance

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Catheter stops draining urine (blockage or kink) – do not attempt to flush yourself; come to ED
  - Fever (>38°C) or rigors – urinary sepsis; require IV antibiotics
  - Heavy bleeding (clots, haematuria) – especially if taking anticoagulants
- **Follow-up:** GP or Urology clinic in 2 weeks if catheter removal planned; if long‑term, district nurse in 1 week for re‑training

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / urology:**
  - Unable to pass catheter despite two attempts by an experienced clinician
  - Blood at meatus or scrotal haematoma before any attempt – do not catheterise; suprapubic route indicated
  - Suspected false passage – stop, deflate, call for help
  - Patient develops severe hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) or NEWS2 ≥5 within 30 minutes – start antibiotics, ECG, IV fluids
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
  - Anaphylaxis to lidocaine (rare – facial swelling, wheeze, hypotension)
  - Cardiac arrest due to severe sepsis or electrolyte disturbance after obstructed uropathy (hyperkalaemia)
